name: Roots of Unity
whatIsIt: The nth ROOTS OF UNITY are the complex solutions of zⁿ = 1. There are EXACTLY n of them, and they sit EQUALLY SPACED around the unit circle, at angles 2πk/n. Two golden facts: for n ≥ 2 they SUM TO 0, and the cube roots 1, ω, ω² satisfy 1 + ω + ω² = 0 with ω³ = 1. They turn 'zⁿ = 1' and symmetric sums into clean geometry.
whyNeeded: Roots of unity crack problems about regular polygons, periodic sums, and symmetric expressions, and are essential in advanced algebra, number theory, and signal processing (the discrete Fourier transform).
realLifeUses: Placing the vertices of a regular polygon on a circle
Adding up evenly-spaced rotations (they cancel to 0)
The maths behind digital signal processing (FFT)
Simplifying symmetric sums in Olympiad algebra

vocabulary.meaning: A complex number z with zⁿ = 1.
All complex numbers at distance 1 from 0.
A primitive cube root of unity; ω³ = 1 and 1 + ω + ω² = 0.

story.title: Points on the Circle
story.text: Nikhil solves z⁴ = 1 and finds only z = 1 and z = −1. 'You're missing two!' says Ma'am. 'Over the complex numbers, zⁿ = 1 has exactly n solutions.' She draws them: 1, i, −1, −i — four points equally spaced on the unit circle. 'And add them: 1 + i − 1 − i = 0.' Nikhil grins: 'The roots of unity are the corners of a square, and they cancel out.'
story.extractedProblem: How many complex solutions does z⁴ = 1 have?
story.answerInStory: 4 — namely 1, i, −1, −i, equally spaced on the unit circle.

props.label: 4th roots of unity: 1, i, −1, −i equally spaced (sum = 0)

visual.caption: The nth roots of unity are n equally-spaced points on the unit circle. For n ≥ 2 they cancel to a sum of 0.

teachingGallery.title: Equally spaced, sum to 0
teachingGallery.note: n points around the unit circle.

props.label: Cube roots: 1, ω, ω² (1 + ω + ω² = 0)

examples.caption: 3 roots form a triangle; they sum to 0.

props.label: 6th roots: a regular hexagon on the circle

standardMethod.summary: For zⁿ = 1, write the n solutions as equally-spaced points at angles 2πk/n (k = 0 … n−1) on the unit circle. Use that they sum to 0 (n ≥ 2) and that ω³ = 1, 1 + ω + ω² = 0 for cube roots to simplify symmetric expressions.
steps: Recognise zⁿ = 1 has exactly n complex solutions.
Place them equally spaced on the unit circle at angles 2πk/n.
Use the sum = 0 fact for n ≥ 2.
For cube roots, apply ω³ = 1 and 1 + ω + ω² = 0.

workedExamples.problem: How many distinct complex solutions does z⁵ = 1 have?
For a cube root of unity ω, what is ω³?
steps: ω is a root of z³ = 1.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking zⁿ = 1 has only the real solutions 1 (and −1).
commonMistakes.fix: Over the complex numbers zⁿ = 1 has EXACTLY n solutions, spread around the unit circle.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking the nth roots of unity add up to n or 1.
commonMistakes.fix: For n ≥ 2 they add up to 0 (equal rotations cancel). Only n = 1 gives sum 1.

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up that z⁴ = 1 has four solutions — 1, i, −1, −i — not just two, and that they sit on a circle as a square and add to 0. Mention the cube roots 1, ω, ω² with 1 + ω + ω² = 0.

revisionCard.summary: Roots of unity: zⁿ = 1 has exactly n solutions, equally spaced on the unit circle at angles 2πk/n. For n ≥ 2 they sum to 0. Cube roots 1, ω, ω²: ω³ = 1 and 1 + ω + ω² = 0. Great for polygons and symmetric sums.

rememberIt.hook: zⁿ = 1 ⇒ n equally-spaced points on the unit circle, sum 0
rememberIt.unpack: Cube roots 1, ω, ω²: ω³ = 1, 1 + ω + ω² = 0. Corners of a regular polygon.

studentQuestions.a: They are equal-length vectors pointing in perfectly balanced directions around the circle, so they cancel — like forces in equilibrium.

studentQuestions.q: What is a 'primitive' root of unity?
studentQuestions.a: One that isn't a root of any smaller zᵏ = 1; its powers generate ALL n roots. For n = 3, ω and ω² are primitive but 1 is not.

formulas.name: nth roots of unity
formulas.remember: n equally-spaced points on the unit circle
